purpose: Defines a code deliverable that exercises one or more Courses on real, runnable, demonstrable code. Each Project ends in a working repository with a README, a runnable build, and an interview claim that the repo underwrites.

project_id.description: Unique atomic identifier per saga numbering convention.

name.description: Slug-style name. Lowercase, hyphenated. Used as the repo name and folder name under 04_Applications.

title.description: Short human-readable title for README and prep documents.
title.example: RiverHorse Gateway Spring -- Spring Boot 3 BFF translation

stack_area.description: Primary stack area. Use cross-stack only when the project genuinely spans 3+ areas as its core purpose.

secondary_stack_areas.description: Other stack areas exercised but not the primary focus.

scope.description: spike = throwaway proof of concept (1-3 hours). small = single feature, single repo (1-2 days). medium = multi-feature, may span repos (3-5 days). large = multi-week. Default to small for KT projects.

type.description: translation = port an existing C# project to the target stack. extension = build on an existing GitHub repo. greenfield = new project from scratch. comparison = same feature implemented two ways for explicit contrast. infra = deployment, pipeline, or tooling project.

anchor_repo.description: If type is translation or extension: the existing GitHub repo this Project anchors to.

purpose.description: One paragraph stating why this Project exists, what it proves, and what it does not try to prove. Distinguish scope from non-scope explicitly.

core_features.description: Bullet list of features the Project must implement to be considered complete. Each feature must be testable.
example: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE against a public IDP
HttpOnly + SameSite session cookie issued to the SPA on successful auth
Authenticated proxy endpoint /api/proxy/{service} that forwards to a downstream resource server with a bearer token
Error handling for token refresh failure and IDP unavailability
Logout endpoint that revokes the session cookie and the IDP session

non_goals.description: Things this Project explicitly does not try to do. Prevents scope creep and clarifies the interview narrative.
example: Production-grade rate limiting (out of scope; would be added by an upstream API gateway)
Multi-tenant token isolation (single-tenant assumed for the demo)

tech_stack.description: Concrete technology choices. Pinned versions where they matter.

linked_courses.description: course_ids of the Courses that feed this Project. The Project is where Course knowledge gets exercised on real code.

deliverables.description: Concrete, testable outputs the Project must produce. Differs from features in that deliverables include non-code artifacts (README, diagram, write-up).

acceptance_criteria.description: How you know it is done.

milestones.description: Ordered checkpoints. Each milestone has a date and a state. Milestones are commit-pinned where possible.

interview_claim.description: The single sentence this Project lets you say in an interview. Specific, falsifiable, defensible by demo or code walkthrough.
interview_claim.example: I built a Spring Boot 3 BFF with Spring Security 6, OAuth 2.0 authorization code with PKCE, HttpOnly session cookies, and a downstream proxy endpoint, deployed to AWS ECS Fargate with infrastructure-as-code.

interview_defense.description: Anticipated probe questions an interviewer would ask about this Project, with prepared answers. Three to five entries.

answer_outline.description: Bullets or a short paragraph. Not a script. The structure of the answer.

questions.question: Why BFF instead of issuing JWTs directly to the SPA?
questions.answer_outline: Tokens never reach the browser. HttpOnly cookies eliminate XSS token theft. Refresh logic stays server-side. The downside is a stateful session, which is acceptable for a low-scale clinical app and can be horizontally scaled with a shared session store.

questions.question: Why Spring Security over Keycloak?
questions.answer_outline: Spring Security 6 with the oauth2-client starter is sufficient for the BFF role. Keycloak adds an IDP that we do not need to host since the demo uses Google. If the engagement required a self-hosted IDP, Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server would be the next step.

blockers.description: Open issues preventing forward progress. Cleared as resolved.

notes.description: Free-form. Architectural decisions deferred, links to forum discussions, todo list.

transitions.proposed_to_scaffolded: Repo is created, build works, first commit pushed. Empty but runnable.
transitions.in_progress_to_milestone_complete: A milestone is reached. Tag in Git. Update status. Move back to in_progress for the next milestone.
transitions.in_progress_to_complete: All milestones complete, all deliverables shipped, README explains the interview claim.
transitions.any_to_blocked: External dependency missing. Add reason in blockers. Resolve and move back.
transitions.any_to_abandoned: Project no longer relevant. Add reason in notes. Never delete the record.

storage.rationale: Definition lives in 05_Wiki because the spec is durable knowledge. Code lives in 04_Applications. Each Project repo can become its own GitHub repo when ready.

example_filled.name: riverhorse-gateway-spring
example_filled.title: RiverHorse Gateway Spring -- Spring Boot 3 BFF translation
example_filled.anchor_repo: private/local .NET 9 ASP.NET Core BFF design (not on GitHub)
example_filled.purpose: Implement the BFF design pattern from a private .NET 9 design as a Spring Boot 3 application. Demonstrates that .NET-to-Spring translation produces equivalent architectural fidelity in OAuth 2.0 and session management. Not in scope: production-grade observability, rate limiting, or multi-tenant token isolation.
core_features: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE against Google as the IDP
HttpOnly + SameSite=Lax session cookie issued to a static SPA stand-in
Authenticated proxy endpoint /api/proxy/{service} forwarding to a downstream stub with a bearer token
Logout endpoint revoking the session cookie and IDP session
Health and info actuator endpoints for ECS Fargate health checks
non_goals: Production rate limiting (handled upstream by an API gateway in real deployment)
Persistence of sessions across instances (in-memory acceptable for the demo)

tech_stack.test_stack: JUnit 5, Mockito, Spring Boot Test slices, Testcontainers for IDP stub

deliverables.description: Spring Boot 3 scaffolding with embedded Tomcat and a single /hello endpoint.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: mvn spring-boot:run starts the app on port 8080; curl localhost:8080/hello returns 200.

deliverables.description: Spring Security with Google OAuth 2.0 client + PKCE.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: Browsing /hello redirects to Google; on return, authenticated user email visible.

deliverables.description: Static SPA stand-in served from /static; calls /api/me through HttpOnly cookie.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: Page shows authenticated user; cookie visible as HttpOnly in dev tools.

deliverables.description: Proxy endpoint /api/proxy/{service} with WebClient and OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: Proxy endpoint returns 200 with downstream payload when called from authenticated SPA.

deliverables.description: Dockerfile, ECS Fargate task definition, ECR push, GitHub Actions deploy.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: git push to main triggers a deploy; the Fargate URL serves the app end-to-end.

deliverables.description: README explaining the .NET-to-Spring translation, the BFF pattern, and how to run locally.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: A senior engineer reading only the README can clone, run, and exercise the auth flow in 15 minutes.

deliverables.description: One sequence diagram of the OAuth 2.0 + PKCE + cookie + proxy flow.
deliverables.acceptance_criteria: Diagram is in README, renders on GitHub, and shows the SPA, BFF, IDP, downstream service, and the cookie boundary.

example_filled.interview_claim: I built a Spring Boot 3 BFF with Spring Security 6, OAuth 2.0 authorization code with PKCE, HttpOnly session cookies, and a downstream proxy endpoint, deployed to AWS ECS Fargate with infrastructure-as-code. The repository runs end-to-end from a clean clone in under 15 minutes.

questions.question: Why BFF instead of issuing JWTs directly to the SPA?
questions.answer_outline: Tokens never reach the browser; HttpOnly cookies eliminate XSS token theft; refresh logic stays server-side. The trade-off is a stateful session, which is acceptable for a clinical app at expected scale and can be horizontally scaled with a shared session store.

questions.question: Why Spring Security over Keycloak?
questions.answer_outline: Spring Security 6 with oauth2-client is sufficient for the BFF role. Keycloak adds a self-hosted IDP we do not need since the demo uses Google. If the engagement required a self-hosted IDP, Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server would be the next step.

questions.question: How does this differ from your .NET 9 BFF design?
questions.answer_outline: Architecturally identical. Spring Security takes the role of ASP.NET Core authentication middleware; spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client takes the role of Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Google. Cookie semantics, PKCE flow, and proxy patterns map one-to-one.

questions.question: What would change for HIPAA-protected data?
questions.answer_outline: Audit logging on every authenticated request with user identity and resource accessed; encryption at rest on the session store; tighter cookie lifetimes; explicit OAuth scopes per data class; PHI-safe log redaction. None of these change the BFF pattern itself.

example_filled.notes: Confirm whether the Elevance Clinical engagement requires Spring Authorization Server or Keycloak as the IDP rather than Google. If yes, MS02 expands to include IDP setup.
